text:
IMPARTIAL NEWS + INTELLIGENT DEBATE

Account

Yusra Mardini fled Syria when a bomb dropped on her swimming pool - now she is an advocate, a two-time Olympian, and hopes France will improve its record on refugees after this summer's Games

Yusra Mardiniwas just 17 years old when she swam across the Aegean Sea with her sister to escapewar–torn Syria. After the engine on their overcrowded boat broke down some 10km off the coast of Lesbos, the duo bravely jumped into the treacherous sea and swam — towing 18 other refugees to safety.

Over one year later, Yusra made history as part of the firstRefugee Olympic TeaminRio 2016, and her incredible story of resilience and determination captured the world’s attention.

Since then, Mardini has continued to break new ground in the pool and as a campaigner. She became the youngest-ever UNHCR (United Nations High Commissioner for Refugees) Goodwill Ambassador, using her platform to address world leaders and international audiences about the refugee struggle. She also competed atTokyo 2020and had a Netflix film based on her life.

This year, the Refugee Olympic Team will return for a third time atParis 2024. So far, 66 athletes from 12 countries, including Syria, Venezuela, and Ethiopia, have received the backing of the Olympic Solidarity programme to get to the games. While Yusra has now retired from competitive swimming, she has a clear message for this year’s hosting country.

“France can do a great job of showing support for the Refugee Olympic Team because they don’t have the best record of hosting refugees,” she toldi.

In 2023, France received a record 142,500 asylum requests but accepted only a third. The same year, the country also adopted a “regressive” immigration bill that takes a tough stance on asylum seekers and migrants.

With the Paris Olympics just five months away, the two-time Olympian is calling on France to “take more refugees after the Olympics.”

“I think they can better their system to include these refugees and encourage everyone in the world to take more refugees,” she added.

Having grown up in Damascus, Yusra knows firsthand the challenges of conflict and displacement in the world of sports. Her adolescent life and training came alongside a war that claimed the lives of more than 300,000 civilians over a ten-year period. When a bomb dropped on a pool she was training in, she decided to flee to Europe.

Almost ten years after her Mediterranean crossing, the number of refugees and displaced persons around the planet continues to grow. The UNHCR expects to support 130 million forcibly displaced or stateless people in 2024. Ongoing conflicts inGazaandUkrainefurther underscore the critical need for international cooperation for those fleeing war.

Reflecting on the interviews held with refugees during the Tokyo Games four years ago, Yusra would like a similar momentum this summer. At a time of increasing global crisis, she hopes this year’s hosts will put the refugee narrative front and centre. The stories, she says, speak for themselves.

“The people that I meet on the Refugee Olympic Team, they’re some of the most inspiring people, some of the most positive people I’ve ever met in my life — even though they carry heavy stories with them,” she said.

“And I think that’s the beauty of the refugee Olympic team. And it will always be.”

Yusra has become a vocal advocate for refugees seeking safety in retirement, and it’s not just France that she thinks can do better.

She also took aim at theUK’s controversial plans to send migrants to Rwanda. Branding the UK one of the “most diverse places” she’s ever been, she emphasised a greater need for compassion.

“A refugee is a refugee; it doesn’t matter where they come from, it is about giving them peace, giving them a chance,” she added. “If everyone opens their door, there will be no crisis because this planet fits all of us.”

More than anything, Yusra wants to use her experience to help unlock sports and education for others fleeing their homes. In December, Yusra invited guests at the Global Citizen Forum to experience a simulation of her own Mediterranean crossing by sitting in a rubber dinghy on the ocean.

Floating on the shores of the Arabian Gulf, Yusra relived her story to a captivated boat of listeners. Shortly after, she hurried to COP 2023 for yet another guest appearance. Having met with world leaders likeBarack Obama, these high-profile speaking opportunities are now second nature to Yusra. But behind it all is a 25-year-old Syrian refugee who was forced to flee a home to which she was deeply connected.

“I look back, and I miss my country every day. I miss everything,” she reflected. “I will forever say this looks like Syria… It’s just where I’m from, and it will always be a part of me.”

“Knowing who you are is something very powerful,” she added, emphasising the strength of her identity as a refugee, Olympian and advocate.

Yusra shoulders a great responsibility by speaking for other refugees and admitted that she can sometimes “get in her head” about the legacy she wants to leave.

“I believe that I was very lucky, after competing at the Olympic Games twice for the Refugee Olympic Team, to have a voice,” she said.

“I know that so many refugees around the world cannot tell their stories and do not have the same platforms, so the more I share my stories, the more I am sharing more stories of refugees worldwide, so that’s exactly why I do it.”

Having dedicated herself to advocacy, Yusra has also had to come to terms with life outside the pool. She’s found an interest in other sports, such as weightlifting and running, and prefers to be a spectator to swimming competitions these days.

“It’s a process, and you grieve [competitive swimming] for a very long time,” she said. “But also you feel happy… I achieved my dream of competing at the Olympics, and I will forever be the swimming girl.”

Impartial news + intelligent debate

title: I'm an Olympian and refugee - I beg France to show compassion beyond Paris 2024
author: Tamara Davison
description: Yusra Mardini fled Syria when a bomb dropped on her swimming pool - now she is an advocate, a two-time Olympian, and hopes France will improve its record on refugees after this summer's Games
